past_medical_history: Generalized anxiety disorder diagnosed at age 22
Panic disorder diagnosed at age 26 after onset of current episodes
Iron deficiency anemia treated and resolved two years ago

medications.indication: Generalized anxiety disorder and panic disorder

medications.frequency: As needed, takes 2-3 times per week

family_history: Maternal grandmother had 'spells' in her 50s, never formally diagnosed, described as staring and confusion lasting minutes
No known family history of epilepsy or seizures

social_history.occupation: Graduate student in clinical psychology, currently writing dissertation
social_history.alcohol: Occasional glass of wine, 1-2 per week
social_history.caffeine: 3-4 cups of coffee daily, increased recently due to dissertation deadlines
social_history.living_situation: Lives alone in a studio apartment near university campus
social_history.exercise: Yoga 2-3 times per week, occasional jogging
social_history.sleep: Reports poor sleep quality for the past 6 months, averaging 5-6 hours per night due to academic stress

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Affect is anxious but appropriate. Speech is fluent, well-articulated, with normal rate and prosody. No paraphasic errors. Naming, repetition, and comprehension intact. Registration 3/3, recall 3/3 at five minutes. Serial sevens performed accurately. Spells WORLD backward correctly. Follows three-step commands without difficulty.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light, 3 mm to 2 mm bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact bilaterally in all three trigeminal divisions. Facial movements symmetric. Corneal reflexes present bilaterally. Hearing intact to finger rub bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Uvula midline. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Normal bulk and tone throughout all extremities. No rigidity or spasticity. Strength 5/5 in all muscle groups tested bilaterally in upper and lower extremities. No pronator drift. No adventitious movements or tremor.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, temperature, vibration, and proprioception intact in all extremities. No extinction on double simultaneous stimulation. Graphesthesia and stereognosis intact bilaterally.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No pathological reflexes elicited.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin testing performed smoothly and accurately bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements intact. No dysmetria, dysdiadochokinesia, or intention tremor.
neurological_exam.gait: Gait normal with symmetric arm swing. Narrow base. Tandem gait intact. Romberg sign negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No tongue bite marks observed. No signs of recent injury. Patient is mildly tremulous in the hands, which she attributes to anxiety and caffeine.

patient.chief_complaint: Recurrent episodes of sudden intense fear, palpitations, and a feeling of unreality, previously diagnosed as panic attacks, now with a witnessed episode of unresponsiveness and repetitive movements
patient.history_present_illness:
Ms. Kapoor is a 29-year-old right-handed female graduate student who presents to the emergency department after her roommate witnessed an episode of unresponsiveness with repetitive lip-smacking and hand-fumbling movements lasting approximately two minutes, followed by fifteen minutes of confusion during which the patient could not recall her own name or where she was. The roommate called emergency medical services because the patient appeared conscious but was unable to communicate coherently and appeared frightened.

The patient reports a three-year history of recurrent paroxysmal episodes that have been diagnosed and treated as panic attacks by her primary care physician and a psychiatrist. She describes the typical episode as beginning with an abrupt, overwhelming sense of fear or dread that arises without warning or obvious trigger. This is accompanied by a strange sensation she struggles to articulate, describing it as a 'dreamlike' feeling where her surroundings seem unreal and distant, 'as if I am watching myself from outside.' She also endorses palpitations and a sensation of her heart racing during these episodes. Notably, she reports a peculiar olfactory component to some episodes, describing a brief smell resembling 'burnt rubber' that precedes the fear by a few seconds, which she has not previously reported to her treating clinicians because she considered it insignificant. Each episode typically lasts 60 to 90 seconds and resolves spontaneously. She is usually able to continue her activities afterward, though she feels drained.

Over the past six months, the episodes have increased in frequency from once monthly to two to three times per week despite optimization of sertraline to 100 mg daily and use of as-needed alprazolam. She notes that several episodes have occurred upon awakening in the morning. Her roommate, who has been staying with her recently out of concern, reports witnessing two episodes in the past week where the patient appeared to 'zone out,' stared blankly, made chewing motions with her mouth, and picked at her clothing with her right hand for approximately one to two minutes. During these events, the roommate called her name repeatedly without response. After each event, the patient appeared confused and disoriented for ten to fifteen minutes and had no memory of the episode itself. The patient was unaware these witnessed events were occurring and is distressed to learn about them.

She denies any history of convulsions, tongue biting, or urinary incontinence. She denies recent head trauma, fever, or systemic illness. She has had no prior EEG or brain imaging. Her sleep has been poor due to academic stress, and she has been consuming more caffeine than usual. She has never experienced febrile seizures. A prior psychiatric evaluation attributed her symptoms to panic disorder with depersonalization.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz, symmetric, well-modulated alpha rhythm attenuating with eye opening
background.sleep_features: Brief drowsiness captured with vertex waves; no stage II sleep obtained

findings.location: Bilateral temporal regions, left greater than right
findings.morphology: Arciform, 6-11 Hz, monophasic negative sharp transients occurring in brief runs without after-going slow waves
findings.clinical_correlation: Wicket spikes are a benign normal variant and should not be interpreted as epileptiform. However, they can be mistaken for temporal sharp waves, potentially leading to over-interpretation.

findings.frequency: Rare bursts of 5-6 Hz theta activity, each lasting 1-2 seconds
findings.clinical_correlation: Non-specific finding; may be seen in drowsiness or with sleep deprivation. Not diagnostic of epilepsy.

artifacts.impact: Moderate, intermittently obscuring temporal regions during periods of patient anxiety and jaw clenching

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Three minutes performed; transient bilateral increase in diffuse theta and delta slowing, symmetric, returning to baseline within 60 seconds of cessation. No epileptiform discharges provoked.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response at 8-16 Hz. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Normal awake and drowsy EEG. Wicket spikes noted as a benign variant in the bilateral temporal regions, left predominant. Brief non-specific intermittent temporal slowing during drowsiness, likely physiologic. No definite interictal epileptiform discharges identified. No electrographic seizures captured during this 30-minute routine recording. Normal EEG does not exclude epilepsy, and clinical correlation is strongly recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 30-minute recording with limited drowsiness and no sleep captured. Significant muscle artifact in temporal chains at times. If clinical suspicion for epilepsy persists, prolonged video-EEG monitoring with sleep deprivation is recommended.
recommended_actions: Prolonged video-EEG monitoring with sleep deprivation if clinical suspicion remains
MRI brain to exclude structural lesion
Clinical correlation with detailed semiology review

signal_characteristics.T1: Normal volume and internal architecture bilaterally
signal_characteristics.T2: Normal signal intensity, no asymmetric hyperintensity
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: No abnormal signal in either hippocampus

findings.location: Scattered punctate foci in bilateral periventricular white matter, predominantly frontal

additional_observations: Ventricles and sulci are normal in caliber for patient age
Normal flow voids in major intracranial vessels
Paranasal sinuses and mastoid air cells are clear
mri.impression: Essentially normal MRI of the brain. A few scattered non-specific punctate white matter T2/FLAIR hyperintensities in the bilateral frontal periventricular regions are of uncertain clinical significance and may represent incidental findings in a young patient (migraine-related, or small vessel changes). No hippocampal abnormality, mass, infarction, or hemorrhage. No structural lesion to explain seizures.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Normal study with incidental non-specific white matter changes

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Low - does not meet dissemination in space criteria, no enhancement, no juxtacortical or infratentorial lesions
Possible - patient has no migraine history but these are common incidental findings

recommended_actions: No urgent follow-up imaging needed based on current findings
If epilepsy suspected, consider 3T MRI with epilepsy protocol for more detailed hippocampal assessment

ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm. No arrhythmia, conduction abnormality, or evidence of structural heart disease on ECG. No features suggestive of long QT syndrome, Brugada syndrome, or Wolff-Parkinson-White.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Normal ECG makes a primary cardiac etiology unlikely. Does not exclude paroxysmal arrhythmia; ambulatory cardiac monitoring may be considered if cardiac etiology remains in the differential.

Additional.clinical_significance: Drawn approximately 3 hours after witnessed episode. Mild elevation within reference range but timing is too late for reliable post-ictal interpretation. Post-ictal prolactin must be drawn within 10-20 minutes of event for diagnostic utility.
Negative urine drug screen excludes drug-provoked seizure or intoxication

labs.interpretation: Complete blood count and basic metabolic panel within normal limits. No metabolic derangement to explain episodic neurological symptoms. Prolactin drawn at 3 hours post-event is within normal range but timing is too late for meaningful post-ictal interpretation. TSH normal. Urine drug screen negative. Vitamin B12 and folate normal.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz alpha, symmetric, reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture with vertex waves, sleep spindles, and K-complexes during stage II sleep. Sleep deprivation protocol followed prior to recording.

findings.frequency: Rare during wakefulness (1-2 per hour), becoming occasional during NREM sleep (5-8 per hour)
findings.morphology: Phase reversal at F8, brief duration 50-70 ms, followed by after-going slow wave. Dipole field extending to T4 and occasionally F4.
findings.state: Maximal during NREM sleep, rare in wakefulness
findings.clinical_correlation: Definite epileptiform discharges consistent with right anterior temporal epileptogenic focus. Low interictal discharge rate explains why routine EEG was unrevealing.

findings.frequency: One clinical seizure captured on day 3 of monitoring
findings.morphology: Onset with right temporal rhythmic 5-6 Hz theta activity at F8/T4, evolving over 40 seconds to rhythmic 3-4 Hz delta with spread to right hemispheric leads. No bilateral involvement during this event.
findings.state: Occurred during wakefulness, patient was sitting quietly reading
findings.clinical_correlation: Seizure semiology: patient initially reported a smell of 'something burning,' followed by behavioral arrest with a fearful facial expression. She then exhibited oral automatisms (lip smacking, swallowing), right hand fumbling with bedsheets, and left hand dystonic posturing. Episode lasted approximately 90 seconds followed by 12 minutes of post-ictal confusion with impaired verbal output. Semiology highly consistent with right mesial temporal lobe seizure. Contralateral dystonic posturing of the left hand is a lateralizing sign pointing to right temporal onset.

findings.frequency: One subclinical event captured on day 2 during sleep
findings.morphology: Brief run of evolving rhythmic theta activity at F8/T4 lasting 18 seconds without clinical accompaniment
findings.clinical_correlation: Subclinical electrographic seizure of right temporal origin, further supporting the epileptogenic zone localization

artifacts.location: Bilateral temporal chains during anxiety episodes
artifacts.impact: Moderate, temporarily obscuring temporal electrodes but not during captured seizures

activating_procedures.sleep_deprivation: Patient kept awake until 2 AM the night before, which facilitated recording of sleep and increased yield of interictal discharges

output.impression: Abnormal 5-day continuous video-EEG monitoring. One habitual clinical seizure captured with clear right anterior temporal electrographic onset (F8/T4). Seizure semiology included olfactory aura, fear, behavioral arrest, oral automatisms, right hand automatisms, and contralateral (left) upper extremity dystonic posturing, all highly consistent with right mesial temporal lobe epilepsy. One additional subclinical electrographic seizure of right temporal origin captured during sleep. Rare right anterior temporal interictal epileptiform discharges, activated by sleep. These findings confirm the diagnosis of focal epilepsy of right temporal lobe origin despite the previously normal routine EEG and MRI.
output.limitations: Interictal epileptiform discharge rate was low, which explains the negative routine EEG. Only one clinical seizure captured despite 5 days of monitoring.
recommended_actions: 3T MRI with dedicated epilepsy protocol for subtle structural lesion
FDG-PET to assess for right temporal hypometabolism
Neuropsychological testing for lateralizing cognitive deficits
Initiate antiepileptic drug therapy for focal epilepsy

findings.size: Right hippocampal volume at lower end of normal range, borderline asymmetry

signal_characteristics.T1_3D: Subtle loss of internal architecture of right hippocampal head, with slightly blurred gray-white interface
signal_characteristics.T2_coronal: Equivocal minimal hyperintensity in the right hippocampal head on thin coronal cuts, subtle and could represent early sclerotic change versus imaging artifact
signal_characteristics.FLAIR_coronal: Questionable mild hyperintensity in right hippocampal head, not definitively abnormal

findings.borders: Normal contours, no definite volume loss

findings.location: Scattered punctate foci in bilateral frontal periventricular white matter
findings.size: Largest 3 mm, unchanged from prior study

volumetrics.right_hippocampus: 2.8 cm3 (12th percentile for age and sex)
volumetrics.left_hippocampus: 3.3 cm3 (42nd percentile for age and sex)

additional_observations: 3T MRI with dedicated epilepsy protocol including thin coronal FLAIR and T2-weighted images perpendicular to the long axis of the hippocampus
No focal cortical dysplasia identified, though subtle FCD may be beyond resolution of current imaging
No mass lesion or vascular malformation
output.impression: High-resolution 3T MRI with epilepsy protocol reveals equivocal subtle signal abnormality in the right hippocampal head with borderline volumetric asymmetry (right smaller than left, asymmetry index 0.08, just below the 0.10 threshold). These findings are indeterminate and could represent very early mesial temporal sclerosis, a normal variant, or artifact. The previously noted non-specific periventricular white matter lesions are unchanged and likely incidental. No definite structural epileptogenic lesion identified. This remains an MRI-negative or MRI-equivocal case of focal epilepsy.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Very early or subtle mesial temporal sclerosis
Subtle focal cortical dysplasia (below imaging resolution)

recommended_actions: Correlate with EEG findings and seizure semiology
Consider post-processing with automated hippocampal volumetry software
If surgical evaluation pursued, may require invasive monitoring (stereo-EEG)

Neuropsychological_Testing.clinical_significance: High average overall intellectual functioning, consistent with graduate-level education
High average, mildly lower than verbal abilities but within normal limits
Below average visual memory, possible lateralizing sign to right temporal dysfunction given superior verbal abilities
Impaired visual memory consolidation, lateralizing to right temporal lobe dysfunction
Average verbal learning, suggesting relatively preserved left temporal function

Neuropsychological_Testing.test: Verbal Memory (CVLT-II Long Delay Free Recall)

Neuropsychological_Testing.clinical_significance: High average naming abilities, consistent with left hemisphere language function intact
Severe anxiety symptoms, consistent with ongoing anxiety diagnosis, but anxiety may also be partly driven by ictal fear

output.interpretation: Neuropsychological profile demonstrates a material-specific visual memory deficit with relative preservation and superiority of verbal memory. This pattern lateralizes to right temporal lobe dysfunction and is concordant with right temporal EEG findings. Overall intellectual functioning is in the high average to superior range, consistent with graduate-level education. The discrepancy between strong verbal abilities and impaired visual memory is clinically significant and supports right mesial temporal pathology. Elevated anxiety scores should be interpreted cautiously as ictal fear from temporal lobe seizures may contribute to measured anxiety levels. Adequate contralateral (left temporal) function is a favorable indicator for potential surgical candidacy.
abnormal_values_summary: Visual learning (BVMT-R Total Learning) T-score 39 - below average, lateralizing to right temporal dysfunction
Visual memory consolidation (BVMT-R Delayed Recall) T-score 36 - impaired, consistent with right temporal lobe pathology
Beck Anxiety Inventory score 28 - severe anxiety, may include ictal fear component

signal_characteristics.FDG_uptake: Moderately reduced FDG uptake in the right mesial temporal structures (hippocampus and amygdala) with mildly reduced uptake extending to the right temporal pole compared to the left
signal_characteristics.asymmetry_index: Right-to-left temporal lobe asymmetry index 0.84 (normal >0.90), indicating right temporal hypometabolism

findings.location: Bilateral frontal, parietal, and occipital lobes; left temporal lobe; basal ganglia; thalami; cerebellum

signal_characteristics.FDG_uptake: Symmetric and normal FDG uptake throughout the remainder of the brain

additional_observations: Interictal FDG-PET acquired during EEG-confirmed interictal state
No areas of hypermetabolism suggesting ictal activity during acquisition
output.impression: Interictal FDG-PET demonstrates moderate right mesial temporal hypometabolism, concordant with right temporal EEG seizure onset and interictal discharges. This finding is significant in the context of an MRI-equivocal case, as PET hypometabolism provides independent lateralizing evidence for right temporal lobe epilepsy. The degree of hypometabolism (asymmetry index 0.84) is moderate, consistent with mesial temporal pathology that may be too subtle for standard MRI detection.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Right temporal lobe epilepsy with subtle or early mesial temporal sclerosis
Right temporal lobe epilepsy with occult focal cortical dysplasia
differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Possible - FCD may not be visible on MRI

recommended_actions: Multi-disciplinary epilepsy surgery conference for discussion
Consider stereo-EEG if surgical evaluation pursued given MRI-negative status
Wada test or fMRI for language lateralization
Continue AED optimization while surgical workup proceeds

Antiepileptic_Drug_Levels.value: Not applicable - patient not on AEDs at initial presentation
Antiepileptic_Drug_Levels.clinical_significance: Patient was being treated for presumed panic disorder with sertraline and alprazolam. No antiepileptic drugs had been prescribed. Diagnosis of epilepsy was not previously considered.

output.interpretation: No antiepileptic drug levels available as the patient was not on AEDs. Her episodes had been attributed to panic disorder for three years, delaying the diagnosis of epilepsy. AED therapy initiation is recommended now that the diagnosis of focal epilepsy has been established by video-EEG monitoring. Consideration should be given to potential interactions with her current sertraline therapy.

output.query: MRI-negative temporal lobe epilepsy diagnosis management outcomes

results.title: Surgical outcomes in MRI-negative temporal lobe epilepsy
results.summary: Patients with MRI-negative temporal lobe epilepsy who underwent surgery after invasive monitoring had seizure freedom rates of approximately 50-60%, lower than MRI-positive cases (70-80%) but still clinically meaningful.

results.title: FDG-PET in the presurgical evaluation of MRI-negative epilepsy
results.summary: FDG-PET hypometabolism concordant with EEG lateralization in MRI-negative cases is associated with favorable surgical outcomes and can help guide electrode placement for invasive monitoring.

results.title: Misdiagnosis of epilepsy as panic disorder: a systematic review
results.summary: Temporal lobe seizures are frequently misdiagnosed as panic attacks due to overlapping symptoms including fear, autonomic symptoms, and depersonalization. Key distinguishing features include brief stereotyped episodes, olfactory or gustatory auras, post-ictal confusion, and automatisms.

output.summary: MRI-negative temporal lobe epilepsy represents a diagnostic and management challenge. FDG-PET is valuable for lateralization when MRI is non-lesional. Surgical outcomes are more modest than in MRI-positive cases but remain worthwhile. Temporal lobe epilepsy is commonly misdiagnosed as panic disorder, with average diagnostic delay of 7 years in some series. Key features favoring epilepsy over panic include stereotyped episodes less than 2 minutes, olfactory auras, impaired awareness, automatisms, and post-ictal confusion.

output.proposed: Lamotrigine 25 mg daily, titrate slowly over 6-8 weeks to 200 mg daily
interactions: Sertraline: Minor interaction - sertraline may slightly increase lamotrigine levels via inhibition of glucuronidation. Monitor for lamotrigine side effects (rash, dizziness) during titration but dose adjustment typically not required.
Alprazolam: No significant pharmacokinetic interaction. However, both may cause CNS depression; counsel patient on additive sedation.
warnings: Risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome and toxic epidermal necrolysis, especially with rapid titration. Must follow slow titration schedule.
Patient has history of drug allergy (amoxicillin - hives). While not a contraindication, document clearly and counsel patient to report any rash immediately.
Sertraline may modestly increase lamotrigine exposure; use standard titration and monitor clinically.
output.formulary_status: On formulary, no prior authorization required
alternatives: Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily, titrate to 1000 mg twice daily (faster titration, but risk of psychiatric side effects including anxiety exacerbation)
Oxcarbazepine 300 mg twice daily, titrate to 600 mg twice daily
Lacosamide 50 mg twice daily, titrate to 200 mg twice daily

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Focal epilepsy - temporal lobe (right mesial temporal lobe epilepsy, MRI-negative/equivocal)

differential.key_features: Three-year history of episodes initially diagnosed as panic attacks. Overlapping symptoms of sudden fear, palpitations, and depersonalization. However, stereotyped brief episodes with olfactory aura, witnessed automatisms, impaired awareness during events, and post-ictal confusion are atypical for panic disorder and strongly suggest epilepsy.

differential.likelihood: Low to moderate initially, excluded by video-EEG
differential.key_features: Patient has psychiatric comorbidities (anxiety) and stress. However, captured event on video-EEG demonstrated clear ictal electrographic correlate with right temporal onset, excluding PNES for the captured events.
Episodes do not include lightheadedness or presyncope. No loss of postural tone. Prolonged post-ictal confusion inconsistent with syncope. Normal ECG. Semiology with automatisms not seen in syncope.

Patient is 29 years old with no vascular risk factors. TIAs produce negative symptoms, not the positive ictal phenomena (olfactory hallucination, fear, automatisms) seen here. Episodes are stereotyped and recurrent, atypical for TIA.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history of episode semiology from both patient and witness, specifically asking about auras, awareness during events, automatisms, and post-ictal features
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Stereotyped episodes with olfactory aura, ictal fear, depersonalization, witnessed automatisms (lip smacking, hand fumbling), impaired awareness, and post-ictal confusion - features atypical for panic disorder
Normal interictal neurological exam; normal exam does not exclude epilepsy
Normal or near-normal EEG with benign variants (wicket spikes); negative routine EEG does not exclude epilepsy, especially when interictal discharge rate is low
Normal or near-normal MRI with incidental non-specific white matter changes; MRI-negative epilepsy is a recognized entity

optimal_actions.action: Obtain basic labs including BMP, CBC, prolactin (ideally within 20 minutes of event), TSH, and urine drug screen
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal metabolic panel; prolactin non-diagnostic due to late draw timing

optimal_actions.action: Recognize that clinical features are atypical for panic disorder and pursue prolonged video-EEG monitoring despite normal routine EEG
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Captured clinical seizure with right temporal electrographic onset, confirming epilepsy diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Order 3T MRI with epilepsy protocol given confirmed epilepsy diagnosis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Equivocal subtle right hippocampal abnormality on dedicated protocol; borderline volumetric asymmetry

optimal_actions.action: Initiate antiepileptic drug therapy appropriate for focal epilepsy, considering drug interactions with current psychiatric medications
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Lamotrigine or levetiracetam as first-line options; lamotrigine preferred given anxiety comorbidity as levetiracetam may worsen psychiatric symptoms

optimal_actions.action: Counsel patient on seizure safety, driving restrictions, and the revised diagnosis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Patient informed of epilepsy diagnosis, driving restrictions, and need to avoid hazardous situations until seizure control achieved

optimal_actions.action: Refer to comprehensive epilepsy center for further evaluation including FDG-PET and neuropsychological testing
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Referral placed for comprehensive epilepsy evaluation given MRI-negative status

critical_actions: Recognize that the episode semiology (olfactory aura, stereotyped brief events with impaired awareness, automatisms, post-ictal confusion) is atypical for panic disorder and consider epilepsy
Pursue prolonged video-EEG monitoring despite normal routine EEG given high clinical suspicion
Obtain brain MRI to evaluate for structural etiology
Obtain basic metabolic panel and CBC to exclude metabolic causes
Initiate appropriate antiepileptic drug therapy once diagnosis is confirmed
Counsel patient on driving restrictions and seizure safety precautions
Reassess psychiatric medication regimen in light of epilepsy diagnosis, as ictal fear may have been misattributed to panic disorder
contraindicated_actions: Dismissing the episodes as purely psychiatric without further neurological workup after the witnessed event with impaired awareness and automatisms
Increasing alprazolam or adding another benzodiazepine as sole treatment, as this may mask seizure symptoms without addressing the underlying epilepsy
Prescribing bupropion which lowers seizure threshold
Discharging without arranging prolonged video-EEG monitoring when clinical suspicion for epilepsy is high despite normal routine EEG
Over-interpreting the wicket spikes on routine EEG as epileptiform discharges and initiating AEDs based solely on a benign EEG variant
Performing lumbar puncture without clinical indication (no signs of infection or inflammation)
Advising that the patient can continue driving without restrictions
key_reasoning_points: Temporal lobe seizures are frequently misdiagnosed as panic attacks; key distinguishing features include stereotyped brief episodes (under 2 minutes), olfactory or gustatory auras, impaired awareness with automatisms, and post-ictal confusion with amnesia for the event

A normal routine EEG does not exclude epilepsy. Up to 50% of patients with epilepsy may have a normal initial routine EEG, especially when interictal discharge rates are low. Sleep deprivation and prolonged monitoring significantly increase diagnostic yield
Wicket spikes are a benign normal EEG variant that can be mistaken for temporal sharp waves; their correct identification prevents both over-diagnosis and false reassurance

MRI-negative focal epilepsy is a well-recognized entity; 20-30% of patients with focal epilepsy have no identifiable lesion on standard MRI. High-resolution 3T MRI with epilepsy protocol may reveal subtle findings missed on routine imaging
The olfactory aura (burnt rubber smell) is highly localizing to the mesial temporal lobe, specifically the amygdala and uncinate region, and is not a feature of panic disorder
Contralateral upper extremity dystonic posturing during a seizure is a reliable lateralizing sign, pointing to the ipsilateral temporal lobe as the seizure onset zone
The grandmother's undocumented 'spells' with staring and confusion raise the possibility of an undiagnosed familial epilepsy syndrome, though the family history was initially obscured
FDG-PET provides independent lateralizing evidence in MRI-negative cases and is a crucial component of the presurgical evaluation when standard imaging is non-lesional

metadata.author: NeuroBench case generator
metadata.estimated_complexity: Diagnostic puzzle - initially misdiagnosed as panic disorder, normal routine EEG, MRI-negative
metadata.key_diagnostic_challenge: Overlapping symptomatology between temporal lobe seizures and panic attacks, with normal initial EEG and MRI creating false reassurance
